title: Reasons for Studying PLs, Evaluation Criteria, and Language Design

questions.question: A senior developer has used only C for their entire career and must now learn Java for a new project. Which reason for studying programming language concepts is most applicable to this developer?
options: Increased ability to express ideas in programs
Increased ability to learn new languages
Better understanding of the significance of implementation
Increased ability to choose appropriate languages for projects
questions.answer: Increased ability to learn new languages
questions.explanation: The developer needs to learn a new language (Java). Understanding general PL concepts — type systems, control flow, OOP principles — means the developer does not need to re-learn fundamental ideas, only the new syntax and library. This is the most direct applicable reason.

questions.question: Which of the following best defines orthogonality in a programming language?
options: A language in which all keywords are reserved words
A language in which a small set of primitive constructs can be combined freely in any way
A language that supports both procedural and object-oriented programming
A language with no undefined behaviour
questions.answer: A language in which a small set of primitive constructs can be combined freely in any way
questions.explanation: Orthogonality means constructs are independent of context — any combination of constructs is legal. The fewer exceptions, the more orthogonal. Fewer constructs with more legal combinations is more orthogonal than many constructs with restricted combinations.

questions.question: A programming language requires explicit type declarations for all variables. Which evaluation criterion is most directly improved by this feature?
questions.explanation: Explicit type declarations enable the compiler to perform type checking — detecting type errors at compile time. Catching errors early directly improves reliability. Writability is slightly reduced (more to type), and readability may be slightly improved, but reliability is the most directly improved criterion.

questions.question: Consider a language without a Boolean data type. Which is the most correct consequence?
options: Readability is reduced because Boolean values cannot be represented
Readability is reduced because Boolean values must be represented in a less natural way
Writability is reduced because Boolean values cannot be represented
Writability is reduced because Boolean values must be represented in a less natural way
questions.answer: Readability is reduced because Boolean values must be represented in a less natural way
questions.explanation: Boolean values CAN be represented (using integers 0 and 1 for example), so 'cannot be represented' is wrong. Using an integer to mean true/false is unnatural and harder to READ — a reader must interpret the numeric value as a logical meaning. Readability is therefore reduced.

questions.question: Which of the following most accurately describes a common trade-off when writability is improved in a programming language?
questions.explanation: Features that make writing programs easier — shorthand operators, operator overloading, implicit type coercions, concise syntax — often make programs harder to read. APL is the classic example: very writable (concise symbols), very poor readability. This readability/writability trade-off is one of the most fundamental in PL design.

questions.question: A language is described as 'strongly typed'. Which statement best describes what this means?
options: All variables must be declared with explicit types
The language uses only static type binding
Type errors are always detected — either at compile time or runtime
The language performs no implicit type coercions
questions.answer: Type errors are always detected — either at compile time or runtime
questions.explanation: A strongly typed language detects ALL type errors. This can happen at compile time (static) or runtime (dynamic), as long as no type error goes undetected. A language can be dynamically typed yet strongly typed (Python). A language can have no explicit declarations and still be strongly typed.

questions.question: Which programming language implementation method offers the best portability?
options: Hybrid implementation (compile to intermediate code, then interpret)
All implementation methods offer equal portability
questions.answer: Hybrid implementation (compile to intermediate code, then interpret)
questions.explanation: Hybrid systems (like Java's JVM) compile source to platform-independent bytecode. The bytecode runs anywhere a virtual machine is installed. Compilation to native code produces platform-specific binaries (low portability). Pure interpretation can also be portable, but hybrid systems combine portability with better performance.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the main factors that influenced programming language design historically?
questions.explanation: The three main language design influences discussed in the textbook are: computer architecture (von Neumann model), programming methodologies (structured, data-oriented, OOP), and application domains (scientific, business, AI, systems). Hardware manufacturing cost is not listed as a design influence.

questions.question: Which programming language category is most closely aligned with the von Neumann computer architecture?
questions.explanation: The von Neumann architecture fetches data and instructions from memory, executes instructions sequentially, and stores results back. Imperative languages mirror this: variables represent memory cells, assignment statements represent data movement, and sequential execution matches the fetch-execute cycle.

questions.question: In a modern computing context, which of the following costs associated with programming languages is generally considered most important?
options: Cost of the language implementation system
questions.explanation: Modern hardware is cheap and fast; compilers are free. The dominant cost is unreliable software: financial losses, security breaches, maintenance of defective code, regulatory penalties. Hardware and compiler costs are trivial by comparison.

questions.question: Type checking performed at runtime increases which cost of a programming language?
questions.explanation: Runtime type checking (e.g., Java's array bounds checking) adds overhead every time the checked operation executes. This directly increases the execution cost of the program. It improves reliability but at the price of execution performance.

questions.question: Which programming domain benefits most from a language with high well-definedness?
questions.explanation: Systems programming (operating systems, device drivers, embedded firmware) runs close to the hardware and depends on exact, deterministic language behaviour. Undefined behaviour in C-like languages is a primary source of critical vulnerabilities. A well-defined language removes ambiguities that could cause security exploits.

questions.question: Which of the following is a disadvantage of pure interpretation as an implementation method?
options: Programs must be recompiled for every target platform
The source code cannot be portable across platforms
Execution speed is significantly slower than compiled code
Debugging support is more difficult to implement
questions.answer: Execution speed is significantly slower than compiled code
questions.explanation: Pure interpreters must decode and execute each source statement each time it is encountered, including re-parsing control structures in loops. Compiled code runs as native machine instructions — dramatically faster. Pure interpretation sacrifices execution speed for flexibility and portability.

questions.question: Which statement most correctly describes the relationship between readability and maintainability?
options: Improving readability increases the cost of maintenance
Improving readability reduces the cost of maintenance
Readability and maintainability are completely unrelated
Readability is a less important criterion than maintainability
questions.answer: Improving readability reduces the cost of maintenance
questions.explanation: Programs spend most of their lifetime being maintained — fixed and extended. Readable programs are easier to understand, so developers can identify and fix bugs more quickly. Higher readability directly reduces the labour (and therefore cost) of maintenance.

questions.question: A language designed for artificial intelligence applications would most benefit from which language category?
questions.explanation: AI applications work with symbolic computation and logical inference — areas where functional languages (like LISP/Scheme) and logic languages (like Prolog) excel. Functional languages support list/symbol processing; logic languages support rule-based inference. Imperative languages are well-suited for numerical and procedural tasks, not symbolic reasoning.

questions.question: Which of the following language features is most associated with improved writability through expressiveness?
questions.explanation: Operator overloading allows writing natural expressions (e.g., complex + complex) rather than verbose method calls. Default parameters reduce the number of arguments programmers must supply. Both features allow more to be expressed with less code — a core writability improvement.

questions.question: Using special reserved words like `begin` and `end` to mark compound statement boundaries (rather than braces) most directly affects which criterion?
questions.explanation: Explicit begin/end markers make block boundaries unambiguous and syntactically visible. There is no possibility of accidentally mismatching or misplacing delimiters. This eliminates a common class of subtle bugs from misaligned braces, improving reliability. Writability is slightly reduced (more to type).

questions.question: Aliasing — where two or more variable names refer to the same memory location — most negatively affects which criteria?
questions.explanation: With aliases, a change through one name silently changes what another name sees. Readers must track ALL names for the same memory location — decreasing readability. Unintended changes through aliases introduce bugs that are difficult to trace — decreasing reliability. Writability may be improved (shortcuts for long names), but readability and reliability suffer.

questions.question: An integrated development environment (IDE) like Eclipse is compared to a command-line UNIX environment with separate tools. Which statement is most correct?
options: The UNIX environment is less time-consuming for most programmers
The IDE is less integrated than the UNIX environment
The IDE is more visual and less time-consuming for most programmers
Both environments take the same amount of time to use effectively
questions.answer: The IDE is more visual and less time-consuming for most programmers
questions.explanation: IDEs combine editor, compiler, debugger, build tools, and other features in one graphical interface. UNIX requires switching between multiple command-line tools manually. For most programmers, the IDE's unified environment reduces time and cognitive overhead. UNIX has its strengths for experts, but IDEs are more efficient for the majority.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the primary design concern for the earliest high-level programming languages (like Fortran I)?
options: Programmer productivity and ease of use
Machine efficiency — producing code as fast as assembly language
questions.answer: Machine efficiency — producing code as fast as assembly language
questions.explanation: Fortran I was designed by Backus specifically to overcome programmer resistance to high-level languages. The main concern was proving that compiled code could match hand-written assembly in speed. Machine efficiency was paramount — programmers at the time would not accept a language that produced slower code than they could write manually.
